subject: PhD Algebra
instructions: Time allowed: 240 minutes. Do seven of the following eleven problems. Please do not turn in more than seven problems. You must show your work. Answers with no work and/or no explanations will receive no credit. State clearly any theorem you use in your proofs. In the problems, \(\mathbb{Z}\), resp. \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{C}\), is the set of all integers, resp. of all rational numbers, of all complex numbers.

problems.text: Let \(G\) be a finite simple (abelian or non-abelian) group of order \(n\). Find the number of normal subgroups of \(G \times G\).
Show that there is no simple group of order \(112\). (Caution: You are not allowed to use Burnside’s \(p^a q^b\)-Theorem.)
Let \(N\) be a minimal normal subgroup of a finite group \(G\). Assume \(N\) is solvable. Show that \(N\) is an elementary abelian \(p\)-group for some prime \(p\).

Let \(A, B\) be two matrices over a field \(F\). Suppose that \(\begin{pmatrix} A & 0 \\ 0 & A \end{pmatrix}\) is similar to \(\begin{pmatrix} B & 0 \\ 0 & B \end{pmatrix}\). Prove that \(A\) is similar to \(B\).
Determine the Galois group over \(\mathbb{Q}\) of

Is there any integer \(d\) such that: for any separable extension \(F\) of degree \(5\) of a field \(K\), if an extension \(E\) of \(K\) is a normal closure of \(F/K\) then \([E:K] \leq d\)? If yes, prove your bound. If not, justify why.
Let \(\xi \in \mathbb{C}\) be such that \(\xi^{2000}=3\). Show that \(-3\) is not a sum of squares in \(\mathbb{Q}(\xi)\).
Prove the Hilbert Basis Theorem: If \(R\) is a commutative Noetherian ring with identity, then so is \(R[x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n]\).
Let \(A\) be a finite dimensional (not necessarily commutative) \(\mathbb{C}\)-algebra with no zero divisors, but with identity. Show that \(\dim_{\mathbb{C}} A=1\).

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: If the ring \(R\) is a principal ideal domain, prove that every nonzero prime ideal in \(R\) is maximal.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: Is the statement in a) still true if \(R\) is not a principal ideal domain? (Hint: Consider the ring \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\).)

problems.text: Suppose that \(R\) is a ring with identity.

subparts.label: a)
subparts.text: Prove that each free left \(R\)-module is projective.

subparts.label: b)
subparts.text: Prove that a left \(R\)-module \(P\) is projective if and only if each short exact sequence of left \(R\)-modules \[0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow P \longrightarrow 0\] splits.
